ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for boiled bitter gourd as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared vegetables is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side-dish serving of boiled bitter gourd is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl of sliced pieces served alongside rice or a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Bitter Gourd (Boiled) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled bitter gourd (F14040024). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.03 g) and vitamin C (33 mg) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11963 (bitter melon, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) as a cross-reference where HPB does not individually report these fractions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — bitter melon / bitter gourd analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for boiled bitter gourd is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 24 (low) is estimated based on the closest analogous entry for bitter melon / bitter gourd in peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN). Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (24 × 4.32) / 100 ≈ 1.0, classified as very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6 mg/100 g) and high potassium (319 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Boiled bitter gourd is highly suitable for hypertension management; its negligible sodium content (6 mg/100 g) and high potassium (319 mg/100 g) support blood pressure reduction, but patients should avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as black bean sauce or oyster sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (approximately 24), low sugar (1.95 g/100 g), moderate dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g), and bioactive hypoglycaemic compounds (charantin, polypeptide-p, vicine)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled bitter gourd is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes; its low glycemic index, low sugar content, and evidence-based hypoglycaemic bioactives support postprandial glucose control, though individuals on insulin or oral hypoglycaemics should monitor blood glucose for additive glucose-lowering effects.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and dietary fibre supporting lipid and vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled bitter gourd is cardioprotective due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium (319 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g); it can be included freely in a heart-healthy diet provided it is not prepared with high-fat or high-sodium sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (33 mg/100 g) and momordicin anti-inflammatory compounds; no solanine or pro-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled bitter gourd is not contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis — it belongs to the Cucurbitaceae family (not nightshades) and contains no solanine; its vitamin C and anti-inflammatory phytocompounds are beneficial, though it does not independently meet the threshold for a positive anti-inflammatory food classification.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) is a minimally processed vegetable dish prepared by boiling fresh green bitter gourd until tender. The vegetable has a distinctive warty, ridged green exterior and a crisp-to-tender texture after cooking. Its flavour is characteristically bitter, which is attributed to momordicin and related alkaloids. It is widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, India, China, and Southeast Asia, commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice or incorporated into stir-fries, soups, and stuffed preparations. Nutritionally, boiled bitter gourd is very low in calories (19 kcal/100 g), low in fat (0.18 g), and provides moderate carbohydrate (4.32 g) with a low glycemic index of approximately 24. It is a notable source of potassium (319 mg/100 g), vitamin C (33 mg/100 g), dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g), and iron (0.38 mg/100 g). Clinically, it is well-regarded for its hypoglycaemic bioactives — charantin, polypeptide-p, and vicine — which have demonstrated blood glucose-lowering properties in ADA-aligned research, making it particularly relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. Its very low sodium (6 mg/100 g) and high potassium content make it suitable for hypertension. Zero cholesterol and negligible saturated fat support cardiovascular health. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and low-calorie dietary patterns.

Can I eat bitter gourd with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14040024) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from the closest peer-reviewed analogous entry (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN) and documented as an analogous estimate. Saturated fat value (0.03 g) and vitamin C (33 mg/100 g) derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11963 as a supplementary cross-reference. All other fields are complete and defensible.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for boiled bitter gourd are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14040024, and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11963) does not provide monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, or trans fat breakdowns for this preparation. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: individual B-vitamin panel and fat-soluble vitamin data for boiled bitter gourd are not reported in HPB Foodo F14040024; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11963 provides partial coverage but does not fully resolve all vitamin fractions for the boiled preparation. phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these mineral values are not reported in HPB Foodo F14040024 and are not fully available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11963 for the boiled preparation. water_g set to -1: moisture content for boiled bitter gourd is not individually reported in HPB Foodo F14040024; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11963 does not provide a confirmed water_g value for this exact preparation. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source — bitter gourd is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. saturated_fat_g of 0.03 g is a supplementary estimate cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11963. vitamin_c_mg of 33 mg/100 g is cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11963. GI of 24 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (2021), AJCN, University of Sydney GI Database framework — no direct boiled bitter gourd GI entry exists in the database. The input record listed 'keto' as a dietary tag; this is noted as borderline given 4.32 g carbohydrate per 100 g — acceptable within strict keto limits only at small portions. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis listed suitable: false but the reasoning described anti-inflammatory benefits; this has been corrected to concern_level: low with accurate clinical framing. Tamil alias 'வேப்பம்பழம் வேகவைத்தது' uses the term for neem fruit; the more accurate Tamil term for bitter gourd is 'பாகற்காய்' (paagarkkaai) — both forms retained in aliases for retrieval coverage; primary Tamil local name reflects common usage.
